figures.caption: Table 2: Representation organization across passes. Probing rows report peak linear-probe accuracy; SAE rows report the peak feature–property correlation r across all learned features. Both methods recover the brackets→rings→atom-level progression from Section 4.1. Per-pass curves appear in Fig. 5.

Table 6: Architecture ablation (block type varies; Standard = learned positional embeddings + GELU; Modern = RoPE + SwiGLU). Each row adds one architectural modification; the final row removes iteration to isolate its contribution. WS halves parameters yet delivers the largest jump. Baselines use H=4 heads to match the weight-shared block, isolating iteration from head count; this differs from Table 5’s GPT-419K (H=2, same embedding dimension and parameter count).

Figure 1: Overview of SmolLM. Top track: for each emitted token, the same 53K shared block runs eight passes. By truncating inference at intermediate passes, we find that the shared block solves grammar in stages, with each stage retained as depth increases: brackets by pass 2, rings by pass 4, valence by pass 8. Bottom track: the model autoregressively emits T SMILES tokens. The molecule at step T is benzimidazole, which is generated by WS-53K-s42 in a 10,000-sample run and absent from the training data.

Table 3: Summary of the head-ablation sweep for WS-53K and WS-206K. Changes are percentage points (pp) from the unablated baseline (n=2,000 per condition for both models). The bracketmatching head is shown separately; all other heads and passes are aggregated. The specific index for the bracket head is seed-dependent (head 2 in s42, head 1 in s43).

Table 4: Architecture configurations, parameter counts, and reproducibility identifiers. Name is the canonical identifier used throughout the paper; checkpoint ID is the matching filename under checkpoints/0504_paper/ on Hugging Face (https://huggingface.co/akhljndl/smollm) and the corresponding wandb run name (entity ajindal, project smollm, tag 0504_paper). Class is the block family (Standard GPT, Modern GPT with RoPE+SwiGLU, weight-shared Modern GPT, or GRU). The -mod suffix on a GPT name marks the Modern block; the -DN suffix on a WS name marks a non-default virtual depth (default D=8); the -8ep suffix marks the 800-epoch training-compute control (default 100 epochs).
Table 5: Validity and FCD across the size sweep (companion to Fig. 2). Validity cells with ± are means over 3 seeds; cells without are single-seed.
Figure 2: Pareto frontier. Weight-shared models dominate unshared GPTs below 1M parameters.

Figure 4: Ablation heatmap (head × pass, change in validity in percentage points; n=2,000 per condition for both models). WS-206K: single hot cell at the bracket head, pass 1. WS-53K: heat spreads across the bracket head, passes 1–3.

Table 7: Per-pass readout from a single trained 8-pass model (WS-206K, n=500 per pass). Same constraint ordering as Table 1 (brackets→rings→valence); absolute validities are head-calibrationshifted. Valid reports the fraction of generated molecules that pass all checks. Brackets, Rings, Valence, and Other assign each invalid molecule to one failure class, rather than counting all possible failures in the same string.
Figure 3: Bracket errors collapse by pass 2, rings by pass 4, valence by pass 8 (companion to Table 1).
